items.title: Modified Dandy criteria
items.contentHtml:
Modified Dandy diagnostic criteria

  • Criteria require cerebrospinal fluid opening pressure >25 cmH2O (in adults) measured in lateral decubitus.
  • There must be bilateral papilledema on fundoscopy; If there is no papilledema, the diagnosis is possible only with palsy of the 6th nerve.
  • Neuroimaging (cerebral magnetic resonance imaging with venography) should be normal without hydrocephalus, mass or venous sinus thrombosis.
  • Exclude other causes of secondary intracranial hypertension (drugs, endocrinopathies, meningitis).
  • There should be no alteration in the level of consciousness or focal signs except paralysis. of the VI pair.

items.title: Opening pressure >25 cmH2O
items.contentHtml:
Cerebrospinal fluid opening pressure

  • The diagnostic threshold is opening pressure >25 cmH2O in adults, measured in lateral decubitus with legs extended and complete relaxation.
  • In children, >28 cmH2O is used (or >25 cmH2O if they are not sedated).
  • Values between 20-25 cmH2O are considered borderline; require clinical correlation and repeat measurement.
  • Pressure should be recorded at the beginning of the lumbar puncture, before cerebrospinal fluid is removed.
  • Falsely elevated pressure can occur due to Valsalva, neck flexion or malposition.

items.title: Bilateral papilledema
items.contentHtml:
Bilateral papilledema

  • Bilateral papilledema is the cardinal fundoscopic finding; it can be asymmetric but rarely unilateral.
  • The absence of papilledema does not rule out the diagnosis if there is paralysis of the 6th nerve and elevated opening pressure.
  • Fundoscopy shows effacement of the edge of the optic disc, edema of the nerve fiber layer and peripapillary hemorrhages.
  • The degree of papilledema is classified with the Frisén scale (grades 0 to 5).
  • The presence of optic atrophy indicates damage. chronic and worse visual prognosis.

items.title: lumbar puncture
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic and therapeutic lumbar puncture

  • The lumbar puncture is diagnostic (measure opening pressure and analyze cerebrospinal fluid) and therapeutic (drain volume to relieve symptoms).
  • The opening pressure should be measured with the patient in lateral decubitus, with the spine in a neutral position.
  • It is recommended to drain 20-30 mL of cerebrospinal fluid to reduce pressure and relieve headache.
  • The analysis of the cerebrospinal fluid should show normal composition (proteins, glucose, cells).
  • Relative contraindication: space-occupying lesion on previous neuroimaging.

items.title: Exclusion of venous thrombosis
items.contentHtml:
Exclusion of venous sinus thrombosis

  • magnetic resonance venography or venous angiotomography is mandatory to rule out cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.
  • Thrombosis can mimic all the criteria for idiopathic intracranial hypertension, but requires anticoagulation.
  • Signs of thrombosis on imaging: absence of flow in the superior sagittal sinus, lateral sinuses or confluence of breasts.
  • Exclusion of thrombosis is part of the modified Dandy criteria.
  • Risk factors for thrombosis: oral contraceptives, thrombophilia, dehydration.

items.foco: Association with obesity and female sex
items.title: Epidemiological association
items.contentHtml:
Association with obesity and female sex

  • More than 90% of patients are women of childbearing age with obesity (body mass index >30 kg/m²).
  • The incidence increases proportionally to the body mass index; Weight loss of 5-10% reduces intracranial pressure.
  • In men or thin men, always rule out secondary causes (venous thrombosis, meningioma, drugs).
  • The proposed pathophysiology includes increased intra-abdominal and thoracic pressure that hinders cerebral venous return.
  • Polycystic ovary syndrome is a common comorbidity.

items.title: Ventriculo-peritoneal shunt
items.contentHtml:
Ventriculo-peritoneal shunt

  • The ventriculo-peritoneal shunt is indicated when medical treatment fails or there is progressive visual loss.
  • It is the first-line surgical treatment; lumboperitoneal shunt is also used as an alternative.
  • Complications: infection, obstruction, overdrainage (headache due to intracranial hypotension).
  • The 1-year revision rate is high (30-40%).
  • It is not recommended as initial treatment except in cases of imminent visual threat.

items.title: First line acetazolamide
items.contentHtml:
Acetazolamide as pharmacological treatment

  • Acetazolamide is the first-line drug; initial dose 500 mg twice a day, titratable up to 4 g/day.
  • It is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces the production of cerebrospinal fluid.
  • Adverse effects: perioral and digital paresthesias, metabolic acidosis, kidney stones, hypokalemia.
  • Contraindicated in allergy to sulfonamides, cirrhosis, severe renal failure.
  • The Topiramate (also carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) is a second-line alternative.

items.title: Irreversible visual loss
items.contentHtml:
Risk of irreversible visual loss

  • irreversible visual loss is the most feared complication; It occurs due to optic atrophy secondary to chronic papilledema.
  • Up to 25% of patients develop permanent visual deficit if not treated appropriately.
  • Periodic evaluation with computerized campimetry and visual acuity is mandatory to detect deterioration.
  • Visual loss may be asymptomatic until advanced stages; The earliest field defect is an increase in the blind spot.
  • The indication for surgery (shunt) is urgent if there is visual deterioration despite medical treatment.

items.foco: Measurement of opening pressure in lateral recumbency
items.title: Measurement in lateral decubitus
items.contentHtml:
Measurement of opening pressure

  • measurement of opening pressure in lateral recumbency is the standard; the patient should be relaxed with the legs extended.
  • The spine should be in a neutral position, without excessive flexion of the neck (avoid false elevation).
  • The manometer should be leveled at zero on the midline of the external auditory canal.
  • It should not be measured in a sitting or standing position because the reference values ​​are not valid.
  • The pressure is recorded before cerebrospinal fluid is removed; The normal value is 10-20 cmH2O.

items.title: Contraindication of corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Contraindication of corticosteroids

  • Corticosteroids are contraindicated in the treatment of idiopathic intracranial hypertension because they do not reduce intracranial pressure in the long term and cause weight gain.
  • They can worsen obesity and intracranial hypertension, in addition to producing osteoporosis, hyperglycemia and immunosuppression.
  • Exception: they are only used in intracranial hypertension. secondary to inflammatory processes (meningitis, tumors).
  • In idiopathic intracranial hypertension, corticosteroids have no place in chronic therapy.
  • This contraindication is a common pitfall in examinations: never indicate dexamethasone.

items.foco: Normal neuroimaging without hydrocephalus or mass
items.title: Normal neuroimaging
items.contentHtml:
Normal neuroimaging

  • Normal neuroimaging without hydrocephalus or mass is an essential modified Dandy criterion.
  • Brain MRI with contrast is the study of choice; should show ventricles of normal size and absence of occupying lesions.
  • Indirect signs on imaging: flattening of the posterior sclera, partial empty sella turcica, distension of the perioptic subarachnoid space.
  • Magnetic resonance venography should be normal to exclude venous sinus thrombosis.
  • Any finding would annul the diagnosis of idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
